text_excerpt: Lords Diary – Baroness Jones of Moulsecoomb Skip to content Baroness Jones of Moulsecoomb Menu Home House of Lords My Bills My Debates My Questions for Written Answer Legislation Crime and policing Employment Rights Football Governance Passenger Railway Services (Public Ownership) Planning and Infrastructure Renters’ Rights Sustainable Aviation Fuel Sentencing Water (Special Measures) Media Reports and essays Podcasts Blog Civil liberties Climate Change Ecology and animal protection Lords reform and PR National insecurity Other issues About Jenny Appointments A Day in the Life Biography In the News Jenny’s priorities Lords Diary Picture gallery Lords Diary january 2024 Lords diary for House magazine NOVEMBER 2022 Lords diary for House magazine October 2021 Lords diary for House magazine Lords Diary in The House magazine: COP26 Edition February 2019 Lords diary for House magazine Thursday I woke up still mortified that I called Matt Ridley ‘that fellow over there’ yesterday in the Chamber. I apologised at once to him and he kindly laughed it off. I usually get to my desk about 8.30am, to keep on top of all the emails and the occasional letter. In the afternoon I raced over to Bermondsey to a small sunny park to introduce the results of the internal Green Party selection for Mayor/Assembly candidates in 2020. Lovely to be with so many Greens. Friday A day off for chores, Astanga yoga, more chores, and the last episode of Game of Thrones, Series 7. Saturday The Climate Strike by school students was amazing. Right up to the moment that pictures started coming out with police horses pushing back the peaceful crowds of children, and the arrests of three young people. They are absolutely right when they say that we politicians have failed them. In the last week I have asked questions in the House on why we are burning so much waste and on how the Government will help farmers achieve zero emissions agriculture and been fobbed off on both issues. There’s not a feeling of urgency about Climate Breakdown and we have to have the situation described to us by schoolchildren. Personally, I’m panicking. Sunday I arrive at 8am at Broadcasting House to be a guest on the BBCR4 Paper Review. The other guests are George Parker from the FT and Shappi Khorsandi, the comedian, with whom I bonded over feminism. Monday An odd week ahead, with a light agenda in the House, so there’s a chance to clear my desktop piling system. I suppose like most peers, I get sent a lot of things, cards, badges, paintings, and I can’t bear to throw them away. My new Hansard intern, Coles Cotter, is doing research on immigration for the arrival (when?) of the Bill. We discussed what amendments might be necessary. Over lunchtime I queued to table an Oral Question on the issue of child spies, which was debated in October last year. I also started sending questions on the same subject for written reply. It’s crucial to follow up on debates, to check on government action. In the Chamber, I tried and failed to get in on an Oral Question on air pollution and its damaging effects, especially on children. My Clean Air (Human Rights) Bill, now waiting for its second reading, would be perfect for the Government to adopt. Tuesday I’ve started doing podcasts called Jen’s Green Jam. My last one was with fellow peer Chris Holmes on diversity in the workplace. I’m now planning my next which will be on Fast Fashion with Amelia Womack, Green Party Deputy Leader. She’s highlighting how environmentally and socially damaging cheap clothes can be, from non-existent labour rights to ocean pollution from plastic microfibres. And we here in the UK are big offenders. At 4pm, a quick interview on College Green for ITV on waste. Medway Council have started to target fly-tippers by confiscating, and in some cases crushing, their vehicles. Very brave. Wednesday News comes in that my friend Molly Scott Cato MEP for the SW has been arrested with other MEPs for breaking into a Belgian military airbase t
